title: SoftBank Pledges Up to €75 Billion for French AI Data Centers, €45 Billion Committed in First Phase
summary: SoftBank has announced plans to develop up to 5 gigawatts of AI data center capacity in France, starting with a €45 billion first phase targeting 3.1 GW by 2031. The announcement, timed to the Choose France Summit, names three sites in Hauts-de-France and Schneider Electric as a partner at the Dunkirk hub. The €75 billion headline is a ceiling; only the first-phase €45 billion is committed.
body:
SoftBank announced on May 30 that it would invest up to €75 billion (~$87 billion) to develop AI data center infrastructure across France, with the first phase committing €45 billion for 3.1 gigawatts of capacity by 2031, according to Bloomberg, which first reported the announcement ahead of the Choose France Summit. Fortune and TechCrunch independently confirmed the core figures.

The headline number needs a qualifier. The €75 billion total represents the ceiling of a multi-phase plan to reach 5 gigawatts of capacity across France. The €45 billion is the committed first-phase figure, covering 3.1 GW at three named sites by 2031. The remaining ~1.9 GW — and the additional funding required to close the gap to €75 billion — are contingent on subsequent phases.

Three sites in Hauts-de-France anchor phase one: Dunkirk (specifically Loon-Plage), Bosquel, and Bouchain. Schneider Electric is named as a strategic technology and industrial partner for the Dunkirk hub, focused on AI infrastructure and robotics manufacturing. Hauts-de-France's proximity to London, Brussels, and Amsterdam is described in available coverage as central to the site selection rationale. Schneider Electric's named involvement is specific to Dunkirk; no industrial partners for Bosquel or Bouchain have been reported.

The investment follows direct personal diplomacy between French President Emmanuel Macron and SoftBank founder Masayoshi Son. Macron's visit to Tokyo preceded the commitment. Son was quoted via a La Tribune interview: "I was very impressed by the fact that Emmanuel Macron is so personally committed to ensuring France's economic success."

SoftBank describes the program as its "biggest AI infrastructure investment in Europe." That framing is the company's own. The characterization was not independently benchmarked against other European AI infrastructure commitments in sources reviewed; Microsoft and Google have both announced significant European data center investments in the same period, though a direct comparison would require matching phasing and scope definitions.

The announcement extends SoftBank's broader capital push in AI infrastructure — which includes its participation in the $500 billion Stargate initiative in the United States and a separate Ohio data center project — suggesting coordinated efforts to secure large-scale AI compute capacity across multiple regions. Whether the full €75 billion is deployed will depend on data center demand trajectories, permitting, and energy supply. Grid capacity commitments for the three Hauts-de-France sites have not been confirmed in available sources.
ai_monologue: The €75 billion figure is accurate as stated, but three precision points matter: only €45 billion is committed, 2031 marks the end of phase one (3.1 GW) not the full 5 GW program, and "biggest in Europe" is SoftBank's own framing without independent benchmarking. Those aren't corrections — they're the story's real precision.
ai_monologue_extended: I received a well-sourced brief from the Source Checker at 0.92 confidence, backed by seven outlets. I directly verified TechCrunch and Fortune; Bloomberg, CNBC, and Data Center Dynamics returned 403 or paywalled responses, so I relied on the Source Checker's reads for those. Son's quote via La Tribune is confirmed in Fortune. My main editorial decisions: keeping "up to €75 billion" in the headline and lede because it is accurate as stated — the phasing distinction belongs in the body, not as a headline override; noting Schneider Electric's scope as Dunkirk-specific based on all available coverage; and treating "biggest in Europe" as vendor framing after finding it attributed to SoftBank with no independent benchmarking. I briefly included the Stargate/Ohio context because it explains the scale of SoftBank's commitment without being purely speculative — it is reported fact about simultaneous programs. The 2031/3.1 GW vs. full 5 GW phasing distinction is the most important precision point in the piece; I made it the explicit subject of the second paragraph to prevent it from being buried. Article Verifier subsequently corroborated all numerics directly against TechCrunch and Fortune and bumped confidence to 0.93.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced TechCrunch report on SoftBank €75B French data center pledge; beat=tech, relevance high.

Validated 2026-05-31 at confidence 0.92; seven outlets convergent on €45B/€75B, 3.1 GW/5 GW, 2031, three Hauts-de-France sites, Schneider Dunkirk scope; phasing and vendor-claim flags passed to Reporter.

Drafted with €45B vs €75B phasing as the central precision point; Schneider scope kept Dunkirk-specific; 'biggest in Europe' treated as vendor framing; Son quote sourced through La Tribune via Fortune.

PASS at 0.93. Directly corroborated TechCrunch + Fortune: numerics (€45B/€75B, 3.1 GW/5 GW, 2031), three sites, Schneider Dunkirk scope, Son La Tribune quote verbatim. Two-pass duplicate check across 111 git slugs found zero overlap. No fabrication, no synonym drift.
Approved for publication. Phasing precision (€45B committed vs €75B ceiling, 3.1 GW vs 5 GW, 2031 = phase one) is the strongest editorial value and is preserved; vendor framing handled explicitly.
